column4: Publish onboarding instructions and internal process documentation
column13:
Hierarchy / linkage
- Current issue: TGTM-1361 — Task — Publish onboarding instructions and internal process documentation
- Direct parent: TGTM-173 — Create vendor/agency onboarding checklist (visible in this extract)
- Grandparent context: TGTM-17 — Set up budget, team, and martech operating model (visible in this extract)

Original intent
Create a clear reference guide for internal teams and external vendors outlining the onboarding process, guardrails, and approval workflow.

Operating context
This item belongs to the Operations domain and supports vendor and agency onboarding operations. The purpose is to make the GTM launch operating model executable, auditable, and easy for internal teams, vendors, agencies, finance, compliance, and leadership to follow.

How we are doing this
- Execute this item as a child deliverable within the parent workstream: Create vendor/agency onboarding checklist.
- Start by reviewing the parent Story objective, current description, existing GTM materials, and any linked finance/compliance/product assumptions.
- Define required onboarding inputs: vendor legal name, service scope, owner, contract/SOW, NDA status, compliance acknowledgement, bank/payment details, invoice format, and approval owner.
- Create a checklist that separates mandatory pre-engagement checks from post-approval setup tasks so teams know what must be complete before work starts.
- Add escalation rules for missing documents, urgent campaign needs, budget exceptions, compliance concerns, and payment disputes.
- Create separate model sections for assumptions, one-time fees, recurring platform/servicing fees, transaction-based fees, white-label pricing, and scenario outputs.
- Keep all assumptions editable and referenced by formulas so leadership can stress-test pricing, conversion, volume, and launch timing.
- Add review checks for formula accuracy, missing assumptions, inconsistent units, and unrealistic revenue ramps.
- Convert the final workflow into a clean internal guide with ownership, timing, examples, links to templates, and a changelog.

Preliminary solution
- Produce a reusable onboarding checklist, intake form, and approval packet for vendors, agencies, and freelancers.
- Document the operating process, owner, inputs, step-by-step execution, review criteria, escalation path, and completion evidence.

Expected deliverable
- A reusable onboarding checklist, intake form, and approval packet for vendors, agencies, and freelancers.

Implementation notes
- Treat this as part of the Blocks Estate GTM operating system for launch execution, with emphasis on vendor and agency onboarding operations.
- Keep the first version practical and usable; do not wait for perfect tooling if a controlled spreadsheet, Airtable, Notion, or Jira workflow can support the process immediately.
- Name the artifact consistently, store it in the agreed GTM operations repository, and link it back to this Jira issue.
- Where the item creates a model, tracker, dashboard, or workflow, include sample rows/examples so reviewers can understand how it will operate in real launch conditions.

Dependencies / inputs
- approved GTM strategy and launch priorities
- current Jira hierarchy and issue ownership
- input from GTM leadership, Finance, Compliance, Product, and Operations
- current budget assumptions, fundraising/timing assumptions, channel plan, finance rules, and baseline spend data
- vendor intake requirements, contract/SOW templates, payment process, tax/vendor forms, and compliance guardrails
- selected tools, account access, tracking plan, data model, UTM conventions, consent requirements, and integration owners

Key risks / controls
- Unclear ownership may cause delayed approvals, duplicate work, or uncontrolled GTM spend.
- Weak data hygiene or inconsistent naming can make reporting unreliable.
- Over-engineering the process may slow execution; the first version should balance control with launch speed.

Acceptance criteria
- The deliverable clearly states owner, users, inputs, process steps, decision points, and output location.
- The artifact is linked back to the parent Jira item and can be used without relying on verbal explanation.
- Finance, Compliance, GTM, and Operations stakeholders can review the artifact and identify required next actions.
- Open assumptions, risks, and unresolved decisions are captured with owners and due dates.
- The onboarding checklist defines mandatory documents, approvals, payment setup, kickoff requirements, and blocked-state handling.
- A new vendor/agency can be onboarded using the checklist without ad hoc instructions.

column4: Document dashboard access, refresh, and maintenance process
column13:
Hierarchy / linkage
- Current issue: TGTM-1357 — Task — Document dashboard access, refresh, and maintenance process
- Direct parent: TGTM-172 — Create monthly budget tracking dashboard (visible in this extract)
- Grandparent context: TGTM-17 — Set up budget, team, and martech operating model (visible in this extract)

Original intent
Record how the dashboard is maintained, who can update it, and what steps are required to keep the data current.

Operating context
This item belongs to the Operations domain and supports monthly GTM budget tracking and spend-control operations. The purpose is to make the GTM launch operating model executable, auditable, and easy for internal teams, vendors, agencies, finance, compliance, and leadership to follow.

How we are doing this
- Execute this item as a child deliverable within the parent workstream: Create monthly budget tracking dashboard.
- Start by reviewing the parent Story objective, current description, existing GTM materials, and any linked finance/compliance/product assumptions.
- Create a budget control table that separates planned budget, committed budget, actual spend, remaining budget, variance amount, variance percentage, and action owner.
- Use the launch scenarios as planning guardrails and define thresholds that trigger leadership review before overspend or scope expansion happens.
- Group reporting by GTM channel, scenario, month, campaign, owner, and vendor so leadership can compare spend to pipeline/outcome signals.
- Map the operational stack across CRM, analytics, email, webinar, landing pages/CMS, support intake, KYC handoff, SEO tooling, and reporting.
- Define naming conventions, data fields, lifecycle statuses, UTM rules, consent rules, user roles, and integration touchpoints before configuration.
- Validate that tracking, ownership, permissioning, and reporting work end-to-end before the tool is released for GTM team use.

Preliminary solution
- Produce a working budget/operations dashboard with owner, source, refresh cadence, and variance commentary fields.
- Use columns for month, scenario, channel, budget owner, planned spend, committed spend, actual spend, variance %, commentary, decision needed, and next action.

Expected deliverable
- A working budget/operations dashboard with owner, source, refresh cadence, and variance commentary fields.

Original intent
Hierarchy/context: Epic: TGTM-16 - Build KPI dashboard and reporting cadence Parent story: TGTM-158 - Track share of voice vs competitors Issue: TGTM-1347 - Define channel and scenario taxonomy Original intent: Set the standard channel list, scenario labels, and mapping rules so all spend is categorized consistently in the dashboard. How we are doing this: Create controlled taxonomy values before data collection starts. Define the allowed categories, naming rules, examples, edge cases, ownership, and change-control process so reporting does not fragment across teams or tools. Preliminary solution: Use a lightweight analytics architecture for Share of voice and competitive visibility tracking: source systems/events -> staging table -> KPI calculation layer -> dashboard/reporting view -> weekly review/action log. The first version should be a controlled taxonomy sheet with approved values, definitions, examples, owner, and version history before the values are pushed into the dashboard model. Implementation notes: - Keep the work tied to the parent story output rather than producing a standalone document. - Capture the metric owner, source owner, refresh cadence, calculation logic, assumptions, and unresolved gaps directly in the artifact. - Where this task affects dashboarding, separate raw inputs, transformed KPI logic, and presentation-layer decisions. - Where this task affects cadence or commentary, make the output usable in the weekly KPI review without additional explanation. Dependencies/data inputs: Primary inputs/dependencies: media monitoring, search visibility, backlink data, social mentions, paid-channel taxonomy where applicable, competitor keyword sets, and manually validated competitor coverage. Related metrics: Blocks share of voice versus PRYPCO, Stake, SmartCrowd, Deed, and comparable real-estate investment platforms across PR, search, social, content, and paid/earned channels. Acceptance criteria: - Output is aligned to the Share of voice and competitive visibility tracking workstream and references the correct parent context. - Metric definitions, data inputs, owners, refresh cadence, and assumptions are documented clearly enough for another team member to reproduce the output. - Preliminary solution is reviewed against at least one realistic data sample or business scenario and gaps are logged as follow-up actions. - The resulting artifact can feed the dashboard, reporting cadence, or funnel instrumentation without requiring a separate interpretation document.

- Define a compliance intake record with submission owner, content/claim type, target market, risk level, reviewer, approval status, due date, decision, and notes.

Hierarchy / linkage
- Current issue: TGTM-17 — Epic — Set up budget, team, and martech operating model
- Direct parent: none; this is a top-level Epic in this extract.

Original intent
Choose the GTM budget scenario, hire or assign GTM roles, implement martech, and create compliance review workflow.

Operating context
This item belongs to the Operations domain and supports martech, CRM, analytics, and GTM systems operations. The purpose is to make the GTM launch operating model executable, auditable, and easy for internal teams, vendors, agencies, finance, compliance, and leadership to follow.

How we are doing this
- Break the operating model into five control planes: budget governance, role coverage, martech systems, compliance approvals, and vendor/agency management.
- Create one shared source of truth for GTM operating decisions so leadership can see what is approved, what is pending, who owns it, and what budget impact it creates.
- Standardize templates, approval paths, cadence, and reporting so each GTM domain can move quickly without losing financial, compliance, or operational control.
- Create a budget control table that separates planned budget, committed budget, actual spend, remaining budget, variance amount, variance percentage, and action owner.
- Use the launch scenarios as planning guardrails and define thresholds that trigger leadership review before overspend or scope expansion happens.
- Group reporting by GTM channel, scenario, month, campaign, owner, and vendor so leadership can compare spend to pipeline/outcome signals.
- Map the operational stack across CRM, analytics, email, webinar, landing pages/CMS, support intake, KYC handoff, SEO tooling, and reporting.
- Define naming conventions, data fields, lifecycle statuses, UTM rules, consent rules, user roles, and integration touchpoints before configuration.
- Validate that tracking, ownership, permissioning, and reporting work end-to-end before the tool is released for GTM team use.

Preliminary solution
- Set up a GTM Operating Model Hub with linked modules for budget scenarios, channel allocations, role coverage, martech stack, vendor onboarding, compliance approvals, and monthly reporting.
- Use Jira as the execution tracker, Airtable/Notion as the structured operating layer where useful, and shared sheets/dashboards for budget and revenue-model calculations.
- Apply one operating rhythm: weekly execution review, monthly budget review, compliance approval SLA review, and leadership decision log.
